subject: Endocrinology
question: A 45-year-old obese man (BMI 32) presents with polyuria, polydipsia, and 8 kg weight loss over 3 months. Fasting glucose 210 mg/dL, HbA1c 9.2%. What is the best initial pharmacotherapy?

options.explanation: Metformin is first-line for type 2 diabetes. It decreases hepatic glucose production and improves insulin sensitivity. Start 500mg BID, titrate up. Combined with diet and exercise. Check eGFR (contraindicated if <30). HbA1c target <7%.

Insulin is indicated if HbA1c >10% with catabolic symptoms (weight loss) or if oral agents fail. This patient can start with oral metformin first. Insulin reserved for severe hyperglycemia or failure of oral agents.

Sulfonylureas (glipizide) are second-line or add-on therapy. They cause weight gain and hypoglycemia. Metformin is preferred first-line due to efficacy, safety, weight neutrality, and cardiovascular benefits.

SGLT2 inhibitors are second-line (add-on) or first-line if cardiovascular disease or CKD. This patient has no CVD/CKD. Metformin remains first-line. SGLT2i added later for cardiovascular/renal benefits.
HbA1c 9.2% requires pharmacotherapy in addition to lifestyle. Lifestyle alone appropriate for prediabetes (HbA1c 5.7-6.4%) or very mild diabetes. This patient needs metformin + lifestyle.

educationalObjective: Initiate first-line pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes: metformin + lifestyle. Consider SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA if CVD/CKD. Insulin if severe hyperglycemia (HbA1c >10%).
keyTakeaway: Metformin first-line for T2DM. HbA1c target <7%. Add SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for CVD/CKD. Insulin if HbA1c >10% or catabolic symptoms.
